title: Yaeji: Dancing in Both Languages
author: Allastair Voss
body:
Kathy Yaeji Lee makes music that sounds exactly like what it is: house music made by someone who grew up between cultures and found a way to live inside that gap.

She was born in New York, raised in Seoul, then Atlanta, then back in New York. Her parents are Korean. She sings in both languages, often within the same song. That's not a stylistic choice. It's just how she thinks.

[spotify:artist/2RqrWplViWHSGLzlhmDcbt]

The early EPs on Godmode from 2017 and 2018 are where she made her name. "Raingurl" became the track people played on repeat, a shuffling four-four groove with her voice floating over the top, speaking in a near-whisper about rain and presence. It sounds nothing like what most people associate with club music, but it absolutely works on a dancefloor. The whisper is the whole point. Club music usually announces itself. Yaeji's early work arrives sideways, slips in through a side door, and by the time you've registered that it's there, your body is already responding.

The production on those EPs was minimal in the way that costs more discipline than maximalism. Every element was there because it needed to be. The kick, the snare, the breath, the reverb on the vocal: these things were making decisions about each other with a precision that producers with far more resources often fail to achieve. The minimalism was not modest. It was exact.

## The Gap Between Languages

The bilingual structure in Yaeji's music is not a parlor trick or a demographic choice. It reflects something real about the experience of living between two languages, two sets of idiom, two ways of understanding what is private and what can be said out loud. Some things in Korean don't translate. Some things in English carry cultural weight that changes the meaning when applied to emotional material rooted in Korean family life. Yaeji moves between the languages because that movement is accurate to her experience.

Listening to her records without understanding Korean is a complete experience. The sound of the language, its vowel shapes and rhythm patterns, functions as texture and emotional signal even when the semantic content is inaccessible. This is not an accident. The voice in this music is not primarily a vehicle for meaning. It is a primary instrument, and the language it uses is chosen for sonic properties as much as semantic ones. The Korean syllables in a given phrase often do something rhythmically that English equivalents could not do. This is compositional thinking, not code-switching for its own sake.

There is also something honest about refusing to resolve the bilingual tension. She does not choose English for the world and Korean for the private. She does not use one language to explain the other. They coexist in the same song because they coexist in the same mind, with equal claim on the thought being expressed. That refusal to hierarchy the languages is itself a position.

## With A Hammer

*WHAT WE DREW 우리가 그려왔던* came out in 2020 via XL Recordings. A mixtape in form but an album in ambition. The bilingual structure is more deliberate there, some songs in Korean, some in English, some in both. "MONEY CAT" uses a playful structure that hides how technically precise the production is underneath. The record also introduced live instrumentation alongside the electronics, foreshadowing where she would go next.

*With A Hammer* (2023) was her biggest statement. She moved away from pure house music into something harder and more physical. The album incorporates breakbeats, trip-hop rhythms, and guitar in ways that feel earned rather than grafted on. "With A Hammer" the title track has a brutalist quality, drums that hit like machinery, vocals processed in real time. The production works live instrumentation into the electronic architecture so thoroughly that the seams disappear. You can't hear where the drum machine ends and the live drummer begins, and that invisibility is the achievement.

"Passed Me By" demonstrates this best: a half-time shuffle with wiry mono synths and a sense of dislocation that is simultaneously musical and emotional. "Submerge FM" from that record moves through three different tempos without losing the emotional thread, a feat of structural confidence that most artists wouldn't attempt. It is the best thing she has made: six minutes that feel like they last both too long and not long enough.

## What the Club Has Always Been For

There's an honesty to Yaeji's music that goes beyond the bilingual thing. She's interested in what it feels like to be in your body in a public space, at a party, on the street, in a room with other people. The club setting is a vehicle for something more internal. The dancefloor in her music is not an escape from interiority but a place where interiority becomes collective, where the private thing that you carry into the room can briefly be shared without words, through the shared physical experience of music in a body.

This is what the best house music has always understood. The genre emerged from communities that needed to make public space for private selves. Yaeji inherits that understanding and extends it. The bilingual element is part of the same project: holding multiple private selves in the same space, letting them coexist without forcing resolution.

Her live sets have evolved significantly since the early DJ sets. The 2023 tour behind *With A Hammer* was widely reviewed as a step-change, more theatrical and more physical, incorporating visual elements that extended the album's concerns into physical space. Audiences described something closer to a theatrical event than a conventional concert.

## The Full Arc

The trajectory from "Raingurl" to "With A Hammer" is worth tracing from beginning to end. It is the story of an artist discovering, one record at a time, the full range of what her initial instincts were pointing toward. The whisper and the four-four groove were not a starting point to be left behind. They were seeds. *With A Hammer* is what happens when you follow what a seed is actually trying to become.

That kind of artistic patience is rare. Most careers accelerate toward the obvious. Yaeji moved toward the true.
